revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:浜王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:HAMA-oji.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Shingu|qid=Q823526|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.717056|N|136.002194|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|qid=Q3200280|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines}}}}
'''Hama Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Hama Ōji == '''Hama Ōji''' (浜王子) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|qid=Q823526|lt=Shingū, Wakayama|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the ancient {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|qid=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} pilgrimage route. The shrine is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11417412}} (designated on January 8, 1959).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2009-03-21}}</ref>
== Overview == The founding date of Hama Ōji is unknown. According to legend, it was established to enshrine the deities {{nihongo|Iine-no-Mikoto|稲飯命}} and {{nihongo|Mikeru-no-Mikoto|三毛入野命}}, who calmed a storm during {{nihongo|Emperor Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|神武東征}} by sacrificing themselves into the sea.<ref name="Nishi">Nishi (1987), p. 167.</ref> This legend suggests that the shrine originally served as a coastal sanctuary dedicated to maritime deities. Over time, it became associated with {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} as a subsidiary shrine (Ōji) worshipping its divine offspring.<ref>Shingū City Board of Education (1990), p. 31.</ref>
The shrine does not appear in early pilgrimage records, such as the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' from 1210, which mentions four Ōji shrines between Shingū and Nachi but not Hama Ōji. The earliest known reference is in the ''Ninety-Nine Ōji-ki'' from 1474.<ref>Shingū City Board of Education (1990), pp. 31–32.</ref> During the Edo period, the ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' described the shrine as a small structure with a torii gate, suggesting it held some significance.
In 1322, {{nihongo|Toshima Kagemura|豊島景村}}, a feudal lord from {{nihongo|Toshima District, Musashi Province|武蔵国豊島郡}} (modern-day {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|qid=Q235130|lt=Kita, Tokyo|1=Kita, Tokyo}}), reportedly transferred the enshrined deity to establish {{ill|Ōji Shrine|en|qid=Q11573004|1=Ōji Shrine}} in Tokyo.<ref>Shingū City Board of Education (1990), p. 32.</ref>
In 1879, the shrine was merged with {{ill|Asuka Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|阿須賀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Shrine (Wakayama)|1=Asuka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11658011}} but regained independence as a village shrine in 1926.<ref>Hasegawa (2007), p. 159.</ref> Before World War II, the shrine was surrounded by pine groves, with its precincts extending to the nearby coastline. This scenic environment was lost after the 1946 {{ill|1946 Nankai earthquake|en|1946 Nankai earthquake|qid=Q10861133|lt=Nankai earthquake (1946)|1=1946 Nankai earthquake}}, which prompted the government to repurpose the land for emergency housing, including parts of the shrine grounds.<ref>Hasegawa (2007), p. 160.</ref>
== Access == * From {{ill|Shingū Station|en|Shingū Station|qid=Q5357415|lt=Shingū Station|1=Shingū Station}} (JR {{ill|Kisei Main Line|en|Kisei Main Line|qid=Q582803|lt=Kisei Main Line|1=Kisei Main Line}}), take a {{ill|Kumano Kotsu|ja|熊野交通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kotsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野御坊南海バス|1=Kumano Gobo Nankai Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11568871}} bus to the "Ōhama" stop, then walk through the residential area.
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Iine-no-Mikoto|稲飯命}} * {{nihongo|Mikeru-no-Mikoto|三毛入野命}}
== Annual Festival == * February 17
== Gallery == <gallery> File:HAMA-oji.JPG|Main Hall (Honden) File:Sacred arch of HAMA-oji.JPG|Torii Gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=Shingū City|year=1937|title=Shingū City History|publisher=Shingū City}} (Republished in 1984 by Shinkō Publishing) * {{Cite book|editor=Shingū City Board of Education & Cultural Property Council|year=1990|title=Cultural Properties of Shingū City|publisher=Shingū City Board of Education}} * {{Cite book|author=Nishi Ritsu|year=1987|title=Guide to the Kumano Kōdō: Survey of the Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Selection 1}} * {{Cite book|author=Hasegawa Yasutaka|year=2007|title=Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage Guidebook|publisher=Shinpū Shobō|isbn=9784882696292}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|qid=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Yunomine Ōji|ja|湯の峰王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunomine Ōji|1=Yunomine Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563413}} – '''Hama Ōji''' – {{ill|Sano Ōji (Shingū)|ja|佐野王子 (新宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sano Ōji (Shingū)|1=Sano Ōji Shrine (Shingu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11385071}}
== External Links == * [http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=8003 Ōji Shrine] (Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office)
{{Shinto-stub}}

|[[湯の峰王子|湯ノ峯王子]] goodbye $1（{{ill|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|en|Kumano Hayatama Taisha|qid=Q335618|lt=Hayatama Taisha|1=Kumano Hayatama Taisha}}） | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[浜王子]] | $1（{{ill|--><!-- | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#小中王子|小中王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#比井王子|比井王子]] | style="background:#ccffcc" |[[九十九王子 (日高町)#松原王子|松原王子]] --><!--Hama Ōji--><!--Hama Ōji--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11557908}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shingu, Wakayama]] [[Category:Designated historic sites of Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shingu, Wakayama]] [[Category:Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Wakayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
